cases.holding: The prosecution's suppression of evidence favorable to the accused that is material to guilt or punishment violates due process — regardless of the prosecutor's good or bad faith. This is the foundational disclosure duty (exculpatory evidence).
cases.proposition_checked: Suppression of favorable, material evidence violates due process (exculpatory disclosure duty).

quotes.text: the suppression by the prosecution of evidence favorable to an accused upon request violates due process where the evidence is material either to guilt or to punishment, irrespective of the good faith or bad faith of the prosecution

cases.notes: The 'upon request' language was later relaxed: under Agurs and Bagley the duty exists regardless of any request. Brady is the constitutional anchor; everything else (Giglio, Bagley, Kyles, Strickler) refines it.

cases.holding: Impeachment evidence falls within the Brady rule: nondisclosure of evidence affecting a key witness's credibility — including a promise of leniency/non-prosecution made to a government witness — violates due process. Knowledge is imputed to the whole prosecutor's office.
cases.proposition_checked: Impeachment evidence (e.g., promises to witnesses) is within Brady; office-wide knowledge is imputed.

quotes.text: When the "reliability of a given witness may well be determinative of guilt or innocence," nondisclosure of evidence affecting credibility falls within this general rule.
The prosecutor's office is an entity and as such it is the spokesman for the Government. A promise made by one attorney must be attributed, for these purposes, to the Government.

cases.notes: Captured examples (inconsistent statements, mental condition, relevant convictions, drug/alcohol issues) are the kinds of impeachment material Giglio reaches. The 'office is an entity' holding is the bridge to Kyles' imputation of police-held knowledge — the LE-relevance hook.

cases.holding: Set the unified MATERIALITY standard for Brady (covering no-request, general-request, and specific-request cases) and confirmed impeachment evidence is within Brady. Evidence is material only if there is a 'reasonable probability' that disclosure would have changed the result — i.e., enough to 'undermine confidence in the outcome.'
cases.proposition_checked: Brady materiality = reasonable probability of a different result; impeachment included.

quotes.text: Impeachment evidence, however, as well as exculpatory evidence, falls within the Brady rule.

The evidence is material only if there is a reasonable probability that, had the evidence been disclosed to the defense, the result of the proceeding would have been different. A "reasonable probability" is a probability sufficient to undermine confidence in the outcome.

cases.notes: Foundational ADD. Defines 'material.' Borrowed the 'reasonable probability / undermine confidence' formulation from Strickland v. Washington. The materiality standard is what Kyles, Strickler, and Benn all apply.

cases.holding: Two load-bearing points: (1) materiality is assessed CUMULATIVELY — all suppressed evidence considered collectively, not item by item; and (2) the prosecutor has an affirmative DUTY to learn of favorable evidence known to others on the government's team, INCLUDING THE POLICE. The prosecutor cannot disclaim knowledge of what the police know.
cases.proposition_checked: Cumulative materiality; prosecution's duty to learn of favorable evidence held by police (the LE hook).

quotes.text: the individual prosecutor has a duty to learn of any favorable evidence known to the others acting on the government's behalf in the case, including the police
its definition in terms of suppressed evidence considered collectively, not item by item

cases.notes: Foundational ADD and THE law-enforcement hook. Because knowledge of police-held favorable evidence is imputed to the prosecutor, officers must surface exculpatory/impeachment material (including their own credibility issues) to the prosecution. This is the doctrinal basis for the 'Giglio cop' problem.

cases.holding: The State may not knowingly use false testimony to obtain a conviction, and that duty applies even when the false testimony goes only to the witness's CREDIBILITY (not directly to guilt). The prosecutor must correct testimony he knows to be false.
cases.proposition_checked: Knowing use of false testimony violates due process, even as to credibility; duty to correct.

quotes.text: The principle that a State may not knowingly use false evidence, including false testimony, to obtain a tainted conviction, implicit in any concept of ordered liberty, does not cease to apply merely because the false testimony goes only to the credibility of the witness.

cases.notes: Foundational ADD (pre-Brady root). The Napue duty (correct known-false testimony) is distinct from but related to Brady disclosure; Giglio expressly builds on Napue. Officer relevance: an officer who knows a witness (or co-officer) testified falsely cannot stay silent.

cases.holding: Canonical statement of the THREE Brady components: (1) the evidence must be favorable (exculpatory OR impeaching); (2) it must have been suppressed by the State, willfully or inadvertently; and (3) prejudice must have ensued (materiality).
cases.proposition_checked: The three Brady elements: favorable + suppressed + prejudice/materiality.

quotes.text: There are three components of a true Brady violation: The evidence at issue must be favorable to the accused, either because it is exculpatory, or because it is impeaching; that evidence must have been suppressed by the State, either willfully or inadvertently; and prejudice must have ensued.

cases.notes: Foundational ADD. The cleanest three-element framework for the page's Rule statement. 'Suppressed … willfully or inadvertently' underscores that Brady is a no-fault duty (good faith is no defense, echoing Brady, 373 U.S. at 87).

cases.holding: Granted habeas relief: the prosecution suppressed BOTH material exculpatory evidence (expert evidence on the cause of the fire) AND material impeachment evidence (the key informant Patrick's ongoing drug use, contradicting his trial testimony) — each independently sufficient under Brady. Applies and recites the three Brady elements and Bagley materiality.
cases.proposition_checked: Illustrative Brady/Giglio case — suppressed exculpatory + impeachment material, each independently material, warrants relief.

quotes.text: Brady clearly established that the defendant must prove three elements in order to show a Brady violation. First, the evidence at issue must be favorable to the accused, because it is either exculpatory or impeachment material.

the state suppressed material exculpatory and impeachment evidence that would have destroyed the credibility of its principal witness, severely undermined its theory of motive, and left it without substantial evidence of premeditation or an aggravating circumstance

cases.notes: Captured as 'Benn v lambert.' Verified correct opinion (Gary Benn v. John Lambert, 9th Cir.) via sub-opinion/cluster 776954. Strong illustration of BOTH Brady (exculpatory) and Giglio (impeachment of an informant) failures in one case. Persuasive only — label as 9th Circuit, illustrative; the binding spine is the SCOTUS line.

corrections: Capture 'Benn v lambert' → Benn v. Lambert, 283 F.3d 1040 (9th Cir. 2002). Verified correct case and holding.
Capture 'Brady and giglio' headings verified: Brady = exculpatory (373 U.S. 83); Giglio = impeachment within Brady (405 U.S. 150). Both citations correct.
No mis-cites among the captured cases; foundational adds (Bagley, Kyles, Napue, Strickler) supplied with full Bluebook cites.
flags: LE-RELEVANCE / 'Giglio cop' is the headline angle: under Kyles, 514 U.S. at 437, the prosecutor is charged with knowledge of favorable evidence held by the POLICE. So an officer's own credibility problems — sustained dishonesty findings, false statements, prior untruthfulness, certain convictions — become Giglio impeachment material that MUST be disclosed to the defense. An officer with such findings can be rendered effectively unable to testify ('Giglio impaired'). Frame this prominently.

Brady is a no-fault, ongoing DUE-PROCESS duty: good faith is no defense (Brady, 373 U.S. at 87; Strickler 'willfully or inadvertently'), it covers BOTH exculpatory and impeachment evidence (Bagley/Giglio), materiality is judged CUMULATIVELY (Kyles), and the duty extends to evidence held by police (Kyles).

Keep Brady/Giglio (a disclosure / due-process doctrine, mostly post-charge trial fairness) conceptually separate from the suppression (exclusionary-rule) doctrine that is the course's core — this is an adjacent Day-2 block, not a Fourth Amendment search/seizure rule.
Benn (9th Cir.) is persuasive/illustrative only — do not anchor doctrine to it; use it as a concrete two-fer example of exculpatory + impeachment suppression.
Distinguish Napue (knowing use of FALSE testimony / duty to correct) from Brady (duty to DISCLOSE favorable evidence) — related but separate due-process duties; Giglio sits at the intersection.
